agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request proposes adding a 'confirmed' status to the Quote lifecycle. The existing domain already defines 'confirmed' in the Order bounded context as 'paid and immutable, queued for fulfillment.' The proposed Quote 'confirmed' means 'verbally agreed but unpaid.' This is a direct collision in the ubiquitous language.","bounded_context":"Quote Management (with cross-cutting impact on Order Fulfillment)","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Confirmed Order","definition":"ESTABLISHED: An order that has been paid and is queued for fulfillment. It is immutable."},{"term":"Confirmed Quote (PROPOSED)","definition":"PROPOSED: A quote where the customer has verbally agreed to terms but payment has NOT occurred. This CONTRADICTS the established meaning of 'confirmed' in the domain."},{"term":"Quote","definition":"A priced proposal presented to a customer, subject to acceptance and conversion to an order."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A commitment to fulfill goods/services, created when a quote is accepted AND paid."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"The term 'confirmed' in this domain means PAID and IMMUTABLE. This is the established ubiquitous language.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Ubiquitous language consistency is a foundational DDD invariant. If the same term means 'paid and immutable' in one context and 'unpaid verbal agreement' in another, every developer, support agent, report, and integration consuming a 'confirmed' status will be unable to distinguish the two states without also checking the entity type. This is a structural corruption of the domain model.","testable_assertion":"The term 'confirmed' must have exactly one semantic meaning across all entities in the domain."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A verbal agreement without payment is NOT a confirmation — it is an acceptance or acknowledgment.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a naming/policy decision. The business could choose any term, but it MUST NOT reuse 'confirmed' given BR-1.","testable_assertion":"A quote with verbal customer agreement but no payment must NOT carry a status named 'confirmed'."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"A quote that has been verbally accepted is still mutable until converted to a paid order.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Until payment occurs, terms can change — pricing may expire, inventory may shift, customer may renegotiate. A verbal agreement is not a binding financial commitment in the fulfillment sense.","testable_assertion":"A quote in 'accepted/acknowledged' status can still be modified, cancelled, or expired. It must not be treated as immutable."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A report queries all 'confirmed' entities across quotes and orders","expected_behavior":"Without disambiguation, this report conflates paid-immutable orders with unpaid-verbal-agreement quotes, producing dangerously misleading data (e.g., revenue forecasts treating verbal agreements as collected revenue)."},{"scenario":"A downstream system receives a 'confirmed' event and triggers fulfillment","expected_behavior":"If a quote emits a 'confirmed' event, integrations built for order confirmation could mistakenly begin fulfillment on an unpaid, non-binding verbal agreement."},{"scenario":"Support agent sees status 'confirmed' and tells customer their order is processing","expected_behavior":"The customer has only verbally agreed to a quote. No payment has occurred. Support agent's assumption is wrong, leading to a customer experience failure."},{"scenario":"Quote pricing expires after verbal agreement but before payment","expected_behavior":"The quote must remain mutable. If the status name implies immutability (as 'confirmed' does in this domain), developers may skip expiration checks."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH RISK — Conflating 'verbally agreed' with 'paid' in reporting or automation could lead to premature revenue recognition, incorrect cash flow projections, and fulfillment of unpaid orders.","operational":"HIGH RISK — Every system, report, and integration that filters on status='confirmed' must now also check entity type. This is accidental complexity that will produce bugs at scale.","user":"MEDIUM RISK — Internal users (sales agents, support, warehouse) will lose trust in status meanings. 'Confirmed' will require constant clarification: 'confirmed-confirmed or just quote-confirmed?'","risk":"CRITICAL — This is domain language corruption. The damage is not in this feature alone but in every future feature, integration, and team member who inherits an ambiguous ubiquitous language.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Sales Agents","impact":"Confusion between quote confirmation (verbal) and order confirmation (paid). May misrepresent order status to customers."},{"group":"Finance / Accounting","impact":"Revenue recognition errors if 'confirmed' quotes are counted alongside confirmed orders."},{"group":"Warehouse / Fulfillment","impact":"Risk of premature picking/packing if fulfillment systems react to 'confirmed' events generically."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Every query, event handler, and status check involving 'confirmed' becomes ambiguous. Technical debt compounds immediately."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"May be told their order is 'confirmed' when only a quote was verbally acknowledged. Expectation mismatch."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction with established domain language. 'Confirmed' is already defined as 'paid and immutable' for orders. Reusing it for 'unpaid verbal agreement' on quotes is a semantic collision. Confidence is low because the feature CANNOT ship as specified without violating the existing domain invariant. The business intent is clear and valid — the naming is not.","high_confidence_areas":["The business need is real: tracking verbal agreement on quotes is a legitimate lifecycle state","The term 'confirmed' is already taken and means something fundamentally different","This will cause cross-cutting confusion in reporting, integrations, and team communication"],"low_confidence_areas":["What the business actually wants to name this state (needs stakeholder input)","Whether there are other Quote statuses already in use that may conflict with a new state"],"assumptions":["The established definition of 'confirmed' for orders is authoritative and in active use","Quotes and orders share reporting surfaces, event buses, or status-based queries","The business intent is to track a real lifecycle event (verbal acceptance) and not to conflate it with payment"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The proposed 'confirmed' status for quotes directly contradicts the established domain definition of 'confirmed' for orders. EXISTING: confirmed = paid + immutable + queued for fulfillment. PROPOSED: confirmed = verbal agreement + unpaid + mutable. These are semantically opposite states sharing the same name.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Agents","Finance","Fulfillment","Engineering","Customers"],"options":["A) Rename the quote state to 'accepted' — customer accepted terms verbally, payment pending","B) Rename the quote state to 'acknowledged' — terms acknowledged by customer, awaiting payment","C) Rename the quote state to 'agreed' — verbal agreement captured, distinct from financial confirmation","D) Proceed with 'confirmed' and disambiguate via entity type (NOT RECOMMENDED — violates ubiquitous language)"],"recommendation":"Option A: 'accepted'. It accurately describes the business event (customer accepted the quote terms), clearly distinguishes from 'confirmed' (which means paid), and naturally leads to the next state: 'accepted' quote → customer pays → 'confirmed' order. This preserves the domain language and the natural business flow: acceptance precedes confirmation."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Reuse of the term 'confirmed' with a contradictory meaning across bounded contexts that share language","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: 'Confirmed' must have exactly one semantic meaning in the domain — paid and immutable. A verbal agreement without payment is not a confirmation."}]}
